questions.question: What are the four stages of compilation?
questions.correctAnswer: Preprocessing, Compilation, Assembly, Linking
questions.explanation: Preprocessing (#include, #define expansion). Compilation (C++ → assembly). Assembly (assembly → object file .o). Linking (combine .o files + libraries → executable). Each stage can be run separately with compiler flags (-E, -S, -c).

questions.question: What does the preprocessor do?
questions.correctAnswer: Handles #include, #define, and conditional compilation
questions.explanation: The preprocessor runs BEFORE the compiler. It handles: #include (copy-paste file contents), #define (text replacement), #ifdef/#if (conditional compilation), #pragma (compiler directives). It doesn't understand C++ syntax — just text manipulation.

questions.question: What is the output of the assembler stage?
options: A text file with assembly code
An object file with machine code and symbols
questions.correctAnswer: An object file with machine code and symbols
questions.explanation: The assembler converts .s (assembly text) → .o (object file). The object file contains: machine code (binary instructions), a symbol table (what this file provides and requires), and relocation entries (addresses that need fixing by the linker).

questions.question: What CPU optimization does Spectre exploit?
questions.correctAnswer: Speculative execution
questions.explanation: Spectre exploits speculative execution: the CPU executes instructions past a branch BEFORE the branch condition is resolved. The attacker trains the branch predictor, then provides an input that causes speculative access to secret data, which leaks through cache timing.

questions.question: How does Meltdown read kernel memory from user space?
options: Speculative execution loads kernel data before permission check completes
By using a side channel in the TLB
questions.correctAnswer: Speculative execution loads kernel data before permission check completes
questions.explanation: Meltdown exploits that Intel CPUs check page permissions in parallel with the speculative load. The CPU speculates through the permission check, loads kernel data into a register, and uses it as an index into a cache-resident array. The permission fault then fires, but the cache state has already been modified — leaking the secret value.

questions.question: What causes the Rowhammer vulnerability?
options: Repeated activation of a DRAM row causing bit flips in adjacent rows
questions.correctAnswer: Repeated activation of a DRAM row causing bit flips in adjacent rows
questions.explanation: Rowhammer: repeatedly activating (hammering) a DRAM row causes capacitive coupling to adjacent rows, flipping bits. ~100,000 activations can flip a bit. Mitigations: ECC memory, increased refresh rate (32ms instead of 64ms), Target Row Refresh (TRR) in DDR4+.

questions.question: Why do drivers need to run in kernel space?
options: To access hardware registers and handle interrupts directly
Because user space is too small
questions.correctAnswer: To access hardware registers and handle interrupts directly
questions.explanation: Drivers must access hardware registers (via MMIO), handle interrupts (register ISRs in the IDT), and perform DMA setup — all of which require kernel privileges. User-space drivers exist (GPU UMD) but still need a kernel-mode component for hardware access.

questions.question: What is MMIO (Memory-Mapped I/O)?
questions.correctAnswer: Accessing device registers by reading/writing to specific memory addresses
acceptableAnswers: accessing device registers via memory addresses
reading/writing memory addresses mapped to hardware
questions.explanation: MMIO maps device registers into the CPU's physical address space. The driver uses ioremap() to create a kernel virtual mapping, then reads/writes to those addresses. The PCIe controller routes these accesses to the device instead of RAM.

questions.question: What is the difference between the top half and bottom half of an interrupt handler?
options: Top half runs in user space, bottom in kernel
Top half is the ISR (fast, minimal), bottom half does deferred work
Top half handles errors, bottom handles data
questions.correctAnswer: Top half is the ISR (fast, minimal), bottom half does deferred work
questions.explanation: Top half (ISR): runs immediately with interrupts disabled, does minimum work (acknowledge interrupt, schedule bottom half). Bottom half: runs later with interrupts enabled, does heavy processing. Split prevents long interrupt-disabled periods.

questions.question: What is the difference between a Type 1 and Type 2 hypervisor?
options: Type 1 is faster, Type 2 is more secure
Type 1 runs directly on hardware, Type 2 runs on a host OS
Type 1 is for servers, Type 2 is for desktops
questions.correctAnswer: Type 1 runs directly on hardware, Type 2 runs on a host OS
questions.explanation: Type 1 (bare metal): hypervisor runs directly on hardware (VMware ESXi, KVM, Hyper-V). Type 2 (hosted): runs as an application on a host OS (VirtualBox, VMware Workstation). Type 1 has lower overhead because there's no host OS layer.

questions.question: What is the approximate cost of a VM exit on modern CPUs?
questions.correctAnswer: 500-1000 cycles
questions.explanation: VM exit (transition from guest to hypervisor) costs ~500-1000 cycles on modern CPUs with hardware virtualization (VT-x/AMD-V). This is why excessive VM exits kill performance — each syscall or I/O in the guest forces an exit.

questions.question: What is paravirtualization?
questions.correctAnswer: Guest OS is modified to make hypercalls instead of executing privileged instructions
acceptableAnswers: Guest OS modified to use hypercalls
modified guest makes hypercalls instead of privileged instructions
guest OS uses hypercalls instead of trapping
questions.explanation: Paravirtualization: the guest OS is aware it's virtualized and uses hypercalls (explicit calls to the hypervisor) instead of privileged instructions that would cause expensive VM exits. Requires modifying the guest kernel.

questions.question: How do Docker containers differ from VMs?
options: Containers share the host kernel, VMs each have their own kernel
questions.correctAnswer: Containers share the host kernel, VMs each have their own kernel
questions.explanation: Containers share the host OS kernel via namespaces (isolation) and cgroups (resource limits). VMs each run a full guest OS with their own kernel. Containers start in milliseconds; VMs take seconds. Containers have lower overhead but weaker isolation.

questions.question: What does Extended Page Tables (EPT) do for virtualization?
options: Adds a second level of address translation
questions.correctAnswer: Adds a second level of address translation
questions.explanation: EPT (Intel) / NPT (AMD) adds a second layer of page tables: guest virtual → guest physical → host physical. The CPU walks both tables in hardware, eliminating the need for shadow page tables and dramatically reducing VM exit overhead for memory operations.

questions.question: What does SMT (Simultaneous Multi-Threading) do?
options: Runs two processes simultaneously on different cores
Exposes one physical core as two logical cores by sharing execution units
Splits a thread into multiple sub-threads
questions.correctAnswer: Exposes one physical core as two logical cores by sharing execution units
questions.explanation: SMT (Intel Hyper-Threading) duplicates the architectural state (registers, APIC) per logical core while sharing execution units, caches, and branch predictor. Two threads can execute simultaneously if they use different execution units. Typical throughput gain: 15-30%.

questions.question: What is CPU affinity?
questions.correctAnswer: Pinning a thread to specific CPU cores
questions.explanation: CPU affinity pins a thread to specific cores via sched_setaffinity(). Benefits: cache warmth (thread's working set stays in pinned core's caches), NUMA locality, and isolation (reserving cores for latency-critical threads like audio). Downside: can create imbalance if pinned cores are overloaded.

questions.question: What is work stealing in the context of thread pools?
options: Threads take work from other threads' queues when idle
The OS moves threads between cores
A thread copies data from another thread
questions.correctAnswer: Threads take work from other threads' queues when idle
questions.explanation: Work stealing: when a thread's own task queue is empty, it 'steals' tasks from another thread's queue (taking from the opposite end to maintain LIFO order for the owner). Used by UE's TaskGraph, Intel TBB, and C++ thread pools to balance load dynamically.

questions.question: What does preemptive scheduling mean?
options: The OS can forcibly take the CPU away from a running thread
Threads are scheduled in order of arrival
The scheduler predicts future thread behavior
questions.correctAnswer: The OS can forcibly take the CPU away from a running thread
questions.explanation: Preemptive scheduling: the OS uses timer interrupts to forcibly deschedule a running thread after its time quantum expires. This prevents any single thread from monopolizing the CPU. Cooperative scheduling (older) relies on threads voluntarily yielding.

questions.question: What is a time quantum (time slice)?
questions.correctAnswer: The maximum time a thread can run before being preempted
questions.explanation: A time quantum is the maximum duration a thread can run continuously before the scheduler preempts it. Typical values: 1-10ms on desktop Linux (depending on HZ and CFS configuration). Too short = excessive context switching overhead. Too long = poor interactivity.

questions.question: What data structure does Linux CFS use to track thread runtime?
options: A red-black tree sorted by vruntime
questions.correctAnswer: A red-black tree sorted by vruntime
questions.explanation: CFS uses a red-black tree keyed by vruntime (virtual runtime). The thread with the lowest vruntime (most 'deserving') is always the leftmost node — O(1) to find. Insertion of newly-woken threads is O(log n).

questions.question: What does EEVDF improve over CFS?
questions.correctAnswer: Deadline-based scheduling within fair-share framework
questions.explanation: EEVDF (Earliest Eligible Virtual Deadline First, Linux 6.6+) replaces CFS. It adds deadline awareness: among threads eligible to run, it picks the one with the earliest virtual deadline. This fixes CFS's latency issues where threads could be starved for multi-millisecond gaps.

questions.question: What is the key difference between a process and a thread?
options: Threads share the same address space, processes have separate address spaces
questions.correctAnswer: Threads share the same address space, processes have separate address spaces
questions.explanation: Threads within a process share the same virtual address space (same page tables, same CR3), same file descriptors, same signal handlers. Processes each have their own address space. This is why thread communication is fast (shared memory) and process communication requires IPC.

questions.question: What does fork() return in the child process?
questions.explanation: fork() returns 0 to the child process and the child's PID to the parent. This is how the code distinguishes which process it's running in after the fork.

questions.question: What is the typical cost of a context switch?
questions.correctAnswer: 1-5 microseconds
questions.explanation: A context switch costs ~1-5 µs on modern CPUs. This includes: saving/restoring registers, switching page tables (CR3 write causes TLB flush on older CPUs), and scheduler overhead. At 4 GHz, 1 µs = 4000 cycles.

questions.question: What is copy-on-write in the context of fork()?
options: The child gets a copy of all parent memory immediately
Memory pages are shared until one process writes, then copied
questions.correctAnswer: Memory pages are shared until one process writes, then copied
questions.explanation: After fork(), parent and child share all memory pages as read-only. When either writes to a page, the MMU triggers a page fault, the kernel copies the page, and marks both copies as writable. This avoids copying memory that may never be modified.

questions.question: What hardware mechanism prevents data races at the CPU level?
options: Cache coherence protocol (MESI) + atomic instructions (LOCK prefix)
questions.correctAnswer: Cache coherence protocol (MESI) + atomic instructions (LOCK prefix)
questions.explanation: At the hardware level, the MESI protocol keeps caches coherent across cores, and the LOCK prefix makes read-modify-write sequences atomic by locking the cache line. The OS and compiler build on top of these primitives.

questions.question: What is the fast path of a mutex acquisition in Linux?
options: A system call to the kernel
A single LOCK CMPXCHG on a futex word
questions.correctAnswer: A single LOCK CMPXCHG on a futex word
questions.explanation: The uncontended fast path uses atomic CMPXCHG (~20 cycles) to acquire the mutex without any kernel involvement. Only when the mutex is contended does the thread make a FUTEX_WAIT syscall to sleep.

questions.question: What is false sharing?
questions.correctAnswer: Two threads modifying different variables that share the same cache line, causing constant cache invalidations
acceptableAnswers: threads on different cores invalidating same cache line
different variables on same cache line
questions.explanation: False sharing: two unrelated variables happen to be on the same 64-byte cache line. When core A writes to its variable, MESI invalidates core B's cache line — even though they're modifying different data. Performance drops dramatically despite no actual data sharing.

questions.question: What does the LOCK prefix do on x86?
options: Locks the cache line for the duration of the instruction
questions.correctAnswer: Locks the cache line for the duration of the instruction
questions.explanation: LOCK asserts a lock on the cache line (or bus on older CPUs) for the entire read-modify-write cycle. No other core can access that cache line during the locked instruction. This is how atomic operations are built.

questions.question: What is the ABA problem in lock-free programming?
options: A thread reads A, another changes A→B→A, first thread's CAS succeeds when it shouldn't
Memory is corrupted during atomic operations
questions.correctAnswer: A thread reads A, another changes A→B→A, first thread's CAS succeeds when it shouldn't
questions.explanation: ABA: Thread 1 reads value A from a shared pointer. Thread 2 changes A→B (frees the old node), then allocates a new node that happens to get the same address A. Thread 1's CAS compares the address and sees 'still A' — succeeds, but the pointer now references freed/reused memory. Fix: tagged pointers (version counter in upper bits).

questions.question: What does CAS stand for?
questions.correctAnswer: Compare-And-Swap
questions.explanation: CAS atomically checks if a memory location contains an expected value, and if so, replaces it with a new value. Returns true if the swap occurred. The fundamental building block of all lock-free data structures.

questions.question: What is the Treiber stack?
options: A lock-free stack using CAS on the head pointer
questions.correctAnswer: A lock-free stack using CAS on the head pointer
questions.explanation: Treiber stack (1986): the simplest lock-free data structure. Push: allocate node, set node.next = current head, CAS head → new node. Pop: read head, CAS head → head.next. If CAS fails, retry. Only the head pointer requires atomic operations.

questions.question: When should you use a spinlock instead of a mutex?
options: When the critical section is extremely short (< ~1000 cycles) and contention is low
When you need to sleep the thread
When multiple threads need to read simultaneously
questions.correctAnswer: When the critical section is extremely short (< ~1000 cycles) and contention is low
questions.explanation: Spinlock: burns CPU cycles (power) but avoids the ~2000-cycle cost of a syscall + context switch. Win when the lock is held for less time than the context switch would cost. Mutex: sleeps the thread, saving power and freeing the core for other work.

questions.question: What is the advantage of a read-write lock (shared_mutex) over a regular mutex?
options: Multiple readers can hold the lock simultaneously
questions.correctAnswer: Multiple readers can hold the lock simultaneously
questions.explanation: shared_mutex: readers acquire shared (non-exclusive) lock — multiple readers can proceed in parallel. Writers acquire exclusive lock — blocks all readers and other writers. This increases throughput for read-heavy workloads where writes are rare.

questions.question: What does memory_order_relaxed guarantee?
options: All operations appear in program order to all threads
Writes are immediately visible to all cores
questions.correctAnswer: Only atomicity — no ordering guarantees
questions.explanation: memory_order_relaxed guarantees ONLY atomicity — the read/write is indivisible. It provides NO ordering guarantees relative to other memory operations. Other threads may see relaxed operations in any order. Use for counters where only the final value matters.

questions.question: What is the difference between acquire and release semantics?
options: Acquire prevents reads from being reordered before the load; release prevents writes from being reordered after the store
Acquire is for writes, release is for reads
questions.correctAnswer: Acquire prevents reads from being reordered before the load; release prevents writes from being reordered after the store
questions.explanation: Acquire: all subsequent reads/writes stay AFTER this load. Release: all prior reads/writes stay BEFORE this store. Together they form a synchronizes-with relationship between threads — the classic mutex pattern.

questions.question: What x86 instruction implements an atomic fetch_add?
questions.correctAnswer: LOCK XADD
questions.explanation: x86: LOCK XADD (exchange and add). ARM: uses LL/SC — load-linked (ldaxr) reads the value, add in register, store-conditional (stlxr) writes back. If any other core touched the address between LL and SC, the store fails and the operation retries.

questions.question: What are Coffman's four conditions for deadlock?
questions.correctAnswer: Mutual exclusion, Hold and wait, No preemption, Circular wait
acceptableAnswers: mutual exclusion, hold and wait, no preemption, circular wait
questions.explanation: All four must hold simultaneously for deadlock to occur. Breaking ANY one prevents deadlock: make resources sharable (mutex is inherently exclusive), require all-or-nothing acquisition (break hold-and-wait), allow preemption (unlikely for mutexes), enforce lock ordering (break circular wait).

questions.question: How does lock ordering prevent deadlock?
options: By ensuring all threads acquire locks in the same global order
By using timeouts on all lock attempts
By detecting deadlocks and restarting threads
questions.correctAnswer: By ensuring all threads acquire locks in the same global order
questions.explanation: Lock ordering: assign a global order to all locks (e.g., by memory address). Every thread MUST acquire locks in ascending order. This breaks the 'circular wait' condition — thread A holds lock 3 waiting for lock 7; thread B can't hold lock 7 waiting for lock 3 because it would have acquired 3 first.

questions.question: What is livelock?
options: Threads are permanently blocked waiting for resources
Threads constantly change state in response to each other without making progress
A thread has lower priority and never runs
A thread holds a lock and never releases it
questions.correctAnswer: Threads constantly change state in response to each other without making progress
questions.explanation: Livelock: threads are not blocked but are too busy reacting to each other to make progress. Classic example: two people trying to pass in a corridor — both step left, both step right, neither passes. Adding random backoff breaks the symmetry.

questions.question: Per the C++ standard, what 4 conditions define a data race?
questions.correctAnswer: Two or more threads access the same memory location concurrently, at least one is a write, accesses are not atomic, no happens-before relationship
acceptableAnswers: concurrent access, at least one write, not atomic, no happens-before
questions.explanation: Per [intro.races]: 1) two or more threads access the same memory location concurrently, 2) at least one access is a write, 3) the accesses are not atomic, 4) there is no happens-before relationship ordering them. The result is undefined behavior.

questions.question: What value does the futex word take when waiters exist?
questions.explanation: Futex word encoding: 0 = unlocked, 1 = locked (no waiters), 2 = locked (waiters exist). The '2' tells the unlocker to call FUTEX_WAKE to wake sleeping threads, avoiding the thundering herd problem.

questions.question: What is the approximate cycle cost of an uncontended mutex lock?
questions.correctAnswer: 20 cycles
questions.explanation: The fast path (uncontended): atomic LOCK CMPXCHG on the futex word, ~20 cycles. No syscall needed. The slow path (contended) costs ~2000+ cycles due to FUTEX_WAIT syscall + context switch.

questions.question: In MLFQ, what happens when a thread uses its full time quantum?
options: It stays at the same priority
It is promoted to higher priority
It is demoted to lower priority
questions.correctAnswer: It is demoted to lower priority
questions.explanation: MLFQ Rule 4: 'Use it and lose it.' If a thread consumes its entire quantum without blocking, it's demoted — assumed to be CPU-bound and doesn't need responsiveness. Interactive threads that block before quantum expires stay at high priority.

questions.question: Which Coffman condition does lock ordering break?
questions.correctAnswer: Circular wait
questions.explanation: Lock ordering (acquire locks in ascending global order) breaks circular wait. Thread A can't hold lock B waiting for lock A because it must acquire A first. The cycle is impossible.

questions.question: Why must condition variable wait() be called in a while loop checking the predicate?
options: To handle spurious wakeups — the thread may wake without the condition being true
questions.correctAnswer: To handle spurious wakeups — the thread may wake without the condition being true
questions.explanation: Spurious wakeups: the thread can wake from wait() even if no thread called notify(). The kernel or hardware may generate spurious wakeups. Always wrap wait() in a while(!predicate) loop checking the condition after waking.

questions.question: What is a counting semaphore?
options: A mutex that allows multiple simultaneous lockers
A counter that blocks when decremented below zero
A semaphore that counts context switches
A debugging tool for lock counting
questions.correctAnswer: A counter that blocks when decremented below zero
questions.explanation: Counting semaphore: holds an integer count. wait() decrements; if count < 0, blocks. signal() increments; if threads are waiting, wakes one. Useful for: resource pools (N identical resources), producer-consumer with bounded buffer.

questions.question: What UE type wraps a mutex with RAII semantics?
questions.correctAnswer: FScopeLock
questions.explanation: FScopeLock wraps FCriticalSection with RAII: acquires in constructor, releases in destructor. Equivalent to std::lock_guard. Automatically unlocks on scope exit (including exceptions/early returns).

questions.question: What is the approximate counter pattern?
questions.correctAnswer: Per-thread local counters that periodically flush to a global atomic total
acceptableAnswers: local counters with periodic atomic flush
questions.explanation: Each thread increments its own local counter (no contention). When the local counter reaches a threshold (e.g., 1024), it atomically adds to a global counter. Reads are approximate but bounded error. UE's STAT system uses this pattern for profiling.

questions.question: What are the two components of HDD access latency?
questions.correctAnswer: Seek time and rotational latency
questions.explanation: Seek time: arm movement to the correct track (2-15ms). Rotational latency: waiting for the correct sector to spin under the head (average = 0.5/RPM, ~2-5.5ms at 7200 RPM). Transfer time is additional but negligible compared to these.

questions.question: What are the three types of page faults?
questions.correctAnswer: Minor (page in memory, just update PTE), Major (page on disk, must load), Invalid (segfault — access not allowed)
questions.explanation: Minor fault: page is in RAM but not mapped (e.g., copy-on-write, shared library already loaded). ~1 µs. Major fault: page must be read from disk (swap or mmap'd file). ~1-10 ms (dominated by disk latency). Invalid: access violation → SIGSEGV.

questions.question: What does the TLB cache?
questions.correctAnswer: Virtual-to-physical address translations
questions.explanation: TLB (Translation Lookaside Buffer) caches recent page table entries. A TLB hit provides the physical address in ~0.5-1 cycle. A TLB miss requires walking the page table (up to 4 memory accesses for 4-level paging), adding ~10-100 cycles.

questions.question: What stores each bit in a DRAM cell?
questions.correctAnswer: A capacitor
questions.explanation: Each DRAM cell is 1T1C: one transistor + one capacitor. The capacitor stores charge (bit=1) or no charge (bit=0). The capacitor leaks (~64ms), requiring periodic refresh that reads and rewrites every row.

questions.question: Why is DRAM read destructive?
options: Reading drains the capacitor's charge, so it must be rewritten
The address lines are damaged during read
DRAM can only be written, not read
questions.correctAnswer: Reading drains the capacitor's charge, so it must be rewritten
questions.explanation: Reading a DRAM cell connects the capacitor to a bitline, draining its charge to measure the voltage. The sense amplifier detects the voltage level, then the value must be written back to restore the charge. Every read is followed by a write-back.

questions.question: What is the difference between static and dynamic linking?
options: Static is faster, dynamic is more secure
Static links libraries into the executable at build time; dynamic links at load time or runtime
Static uses more memory; dynamic uses more disk
questions.correctAnswer: Static links libraries into the executable at build time; dynamic links at load time or runtime
questions.explanation: Static linking: library code is copied into the executable at build time. Larger binary, no external dependencies. Dynamic linking: executable references shared libraries (.so/.dll); the linker resolves symbols at load time. Smaller binary, shared across processes.

questions.question: What CPU instruction triggers a system call on x86-64?
questions.correctAnswer: SYSCALL
questions.explanation: x86-64 uses SYSCALL (Intel) or SYSCALL (AMD — identical). It switches the CPU from ring 3 (user mode) to ring 0 (kernel mode), saves RIP to RCX and RFLAGS to R11, and jumps to the address in the LSTAR MSR.

questions.question: What is the page cache?
options: The kernel's cache of disk blocks in unused RAM
A cache of virtual memory pages
questions.correctAnswer: The kernel's cache of disk blocks in unused RAM
questions.explanation: The page cache stores recently-read disk blocks in RAM. When a file is read, the kernel first checks the page cache. If found (cache hit), no disk I/O needed. 'Unused' RAM is actually used for page cache — it's reclaimed when applications need memory. This is why 'free -m' on Linux shows most RAM as 'available' (used for cache).

questions.question: What is the maximum throughput of PCIe 4.0 x4 (typical NVMe configuration)?
questions.correctAnswer: 8 GB/s
questions.explanation: PCIe 4.0: 16 GT/s per lane, 128b/130b encoding → ~2 GB/s per lane. ×4 lanes = ~8 GB/s. PCIe 5.0 doubles this to ~16 GB/s (×4 lanes). Compare: SATA III = 0.6 GB/s.

questions.question: Why must SSDs erase entire blocks before writing?
options: NAND flash cells can only be programmed from 1→0; to write 1, the entire block must be erased
questions.correctAnswer: NAND flash cells can only be programmed from 1→0; to write 1, the entire block must be erased
questions.explanation: NAND flash: programming changes bits 1→0. To change a 0 back to 1, the entire erase block (hundreds of pages) must be erased (reset all to 1). This is why SSDs need garbage collection — they can't overwrite in place.

questions.question: How large is a 2MB huge page compared to a standard 4KB page?
questions.correctAnswer: 512 times larger
questions.explanation: 2MB / 4KB = 512. A 2MB huge page covers the same address range as 512 standard pages but uses a single TLB entry. This dramatically reduces TLB misses for large-memory workloads (databases, VMs, game engines).

questions.question: What does PCB stand for in OS context?
questions.correctAnswer: Process Control Block
questions.explanation: The PCB (Process Control Block) is the kernel's data structure representing a process. Contains: PID, process state, saved register context (for context switch), memory management info (page tables), open file descriptors, scheduling priority.

questions.question: What is a stack canary?
options: A random value placed before the return address to detect buffer overflows
questions.correctAnswer: A random value placed before the return address to detect buffer overflows
questions.explanation: Stack canary: the compiler places a random value between local variables and the saved return address. Before RET, the canary is checked. If overwritten (buffer overflow), the program aborts instead of jumping to attacker-controlled address. Enabled by -fstack-protector.

questions.question: In the System V AMD64 calling convention, where are the first 6 integer arguments passed?
questions.correctAnswer: RDI, RSI, RDX, RCX, R8, R9
acceptableAnswers: rdi rsi rdx rcx r8 r9
RDI RSI RDX RCX R8 R9
questions.explanation: System V AMD64 (Linux): first 6 integer args in RDI, RSI, RDX, RCX, R8, R9. Return value in RAX. Additional args on stack. Windows x64 uses different convention: RCX, RDX, R8, R9.

questions.question: What does the x86 CALL instruction do?
options: Pushes return address onto stack, then jumps
questions.correctAnswer: Pushes return address onto stack, then jumps
questions.explanation: CALL pushes RIP (the address of the next instruction) onto the stack and jumps to the target. RET pops the saved RIP from the stack and jumps there. Together they implement function call/return.

questions.question: Which register is the stack pointer on x86-64?
questions.correctAnswer: RSP
questions.explanation: RSP (Stack Pointer) points to the top of the stack. PUSH decrements RSP and writes; POP reads and increments RSP. RBP (Base Pointer) traditionally marks the frame base, but modern code often omits it (-fomit-frame-pointer).

questions.question: What problem does using quaternions for rotation avoid?
questions.correctAnswer: Gimbal lock
questions.explanation: Euler angles (pitch, yaw, roll) suffer from gimbal lock: when one axis aligns with another, you lose a degree of freedom. Quaternions represent rotation as a 4D unit vector, avoiding this entirely.

questions.question: What is Slerp?
options: Spherical Linear Interpolation between two quaternions
questions.correctAnswer: Spherical Linear Interpolation between two quaternions
questions.explanation: Slerp interpolates between two quaternions along the shortest path on the 4D hypersphere. Produces constant angular velocity — each interpolation step rotates by the same angle. Used for smooth camera transitions and animation blending.

questions.question: In a 4×4 homogeneous transformation matrix, where is the translation stored?
questions.correctAnswer: Last column (column 4)
questions.explanation: In a 4×4 transformation matrix, column 4 stores the translation (x, y, z, 1). The upper-left 3×3 stores rotation and scale. The bottom row is typically (0, 0, 0, 1).

questions.question: Why is matrix multiplication not commutative?
options: Order of transformations matters — rotate then translate ≠ translate then rotate
questions.correctAnswer: Order of transformations matters — rotate then translate ≠ translate then rotate
questions.explanation: AB ≠ BA because the order of transformations changes the result. Rotating then translating moves the rotated object. Translating then rotating orbits the object around the origin. Matrix multiplication composes transformations right-to-left.

questions.question: What is the dot product of two perpendicular vectors?
questions.explanation: Dot product: A·B = |A||B|cos(θ). When θ = 90°, cos(90°) = 0, so A·B = 0. The dot product is zero when vectors are perpendicular.

questions.question: What does the cross product of two 3D vectors produce?
options: A vector perpendicular to both inputs
questions.correctAnswer: A vector perpendicular to both inputs
questions.explanation: Cross product A×B produces a vector perpendicular to both A and B, with magnitude |A||B|sin(θ). Direction follows the right-hand rule. Used for: surface normals, torque calculation, determining 'left' vs 'right' of a direction.

questions.question: What is normalization?
options: Making a vector's components all equal
Scaling a vector to have length 1
questions.correctAnswer: Scaling a vector to have length 1
questions.explanation: Normalization: dividing each component by the vector's magnitude. Result is a unit vector pointing in the same direction. Used for: direction vectors, surface normals, lighting calculations.

questions.question: What problem does CCD solve?
questions.correctAnswer: Tunneling — fast objects passing through thin obstacles between frames
questions.explanation: Discrete collision detection checks for overlap at discrete frames. A bullet moving 10m/frame can pass completely through a 0.1m wall without ever overlapping at a frame boundary. CCD sweeps the object's motion to find the exact time of impact (TOI).

questions.question: What is the purpose of Baumgarte stabilization in physics simulation?
options: To correct position drift caused by numerical error
questions.correctAnswer: To correct position drift caused by numerical error
questions.explanation: Baumgarte stabilization adds a position correction term to the velocity-level constraint solve. Without it, small numerical errors accumulate and objects slowly sink into each other. The factor β (0.1-0.3) controls how much penetration is corrected per frame.

questions.question: Why does explicit Euler integration gain energy over time?
options: It assumes constant acceleration over the timestep, overshooting the true curve
questions.correctAnswer: It assumes constant acceleration over the timestep, overshooting the true curve
questions.explanation: Euler: x(t+dt) = x(t) + v(t)·dt. Uses current velocity for the entire step, but velocity changes during the step. The straight-line approximation always overshoots a curved path, adding energy. Semi-implicit Euler fixes this by using the NEW velocity for position.

questions.question: What does the inertia tensor describe?
options: How mass is distributed around the center of mass — resistance to rotation
questions.correctAnswer: How mass is distributed around the center of mass — resistance to rotation
questions.explanation: The inertia tensor is a 3×3 matrix describing an object's resistance to angular acceleration around each axis. A long thin box has low inertia around its long axis (easy to spin) and high inertia around its short axis (hard to spin).

questions.question: What does GJK operate on to test intersection?
questions.correctAnswer: The Minkowski difference (A - B)
questions.explanation: GJK computes the Minkowski difference A⊖B = {a-b : a∈A, b∈B}. A and B intersect iff the origin (0,0,0) is inside A⊖B. GJK iteratively builds a simplex inside A⊖B, checking if it contains the origin.

questions.question: How many axes must be tested for SAT between two oriented boxes (OBBs)?
questions.explanation: OBB-OBB SAT: 3 face normals of A + 3 face normals of B + 9 edge cross products (3×3) = 15 total axes. If projection intervals don't overlap on ANY axis, the boxes are separated.

questions.question: What is the parametric form of a ray?
questions.correctAnswer: P(t) = origin + t × direction, where t ≥ 0
questions.explanation: A ray: P(t) = O + t·D, t ≥ 0. O = origin point, D = direction vector. t = 0 gives the origin, increasing t moves along the ray. Used for: hit-scan weapons, visibility checks, ray tracing.

questions.question: How do you check if two AABBs overlap?
options: Check if centers are within radius
Check if their intervals overlap on all 3 axes
questions.correctAnswer: Check if their intervals overlap on all 3 axes
questions.explanation: AABB overlap test: for each axis (x, y, z), check if [minA, maxA] overlaps [minB, maxB]. If intervals overlap on ALL three axes, the AABBs overlap. O(1) operations — the fastest collision test.

questions.question: How does estimation error scale with the number of Monte Carlo samples?
questions.correctAnswer: As 1/√N — quadruple samples, half error
questions.explanation: Monte Carlo error ∝ 1/√N. To halve the noise, you need 4× more samples. To reduce noise to 10%, you need 100× more samples. This is why real-time path tracing (1 sample per pixel) is noisy without denoising (DLSS Ray Reconstruction).

questions.question: How many coefficients are needed for diffuse irradiance with 3-band SH?
questions.explanation: 3-band SH (l=0,1,2) requires 9 coefficients per color channel (27 total for RGB). This encodes environment lighting compactly — a 72MB cube map becomes 108 bytes. Used for UE SkyLight and Unity Light Probes.

questions.question: What does the Fresnel effect describe?
options: How light diffuses on rough surfaces
How reflectivity increases at grazing angles
questions.correctAnswer: How reflectivity increases at grazing angles
questions.explanation: Fresnel: surfaces become more reflective at shallow viewing angles. A pool of water: looking straight down (0°) — you see through it. Looking across the surface (near 90°) — you see strong reflection. Schlick's approximation is the common real-time implementation.

questions.question: What do barycentric coordinates sum to for any point inside a triangle?
questions.explanation: Barycentric coordinates (α, β, γ) represent any point in a triangle as α·V0 + β·V1 + γ·V2 where α+β+γ=1. The GPU rasterizer uses these to interpolate vertex attributes (color, UV, normal) across triangle surfaces.

questions.question: What is the purpose of the perspective divide (w-divide)?
options: To make distant objects appear smaller
To convert screen coordinates to world
questions.correctAnswer: To make distant objects appear smaller
questions.explanation: After the projection matrix, clip-space coordinates have w = -z (eye-space depth). Dividing x,y,z by w produces normalized device coordinates where distant objects (large |z|) become smaller — perspective projection.

questions.question: What is the first step in Recast navmesh generation?
questions.correctAnswer: Voxelization of the world geometry
questions.explanation: Recast pipeline: 1) Voxelization — convert world geometry into 3D grid. 2) Filter walkable surfaces (slope, headroom). 3) Watershed region partitioning. 4) Contour extraction. 5) Polygon triangulation. 6) Detail mesh.

questions.question: What three rules produce flocking behavior (boids)?
questions.correctAnswer: Separation, alignment, cohesion
questions.explanation: Reynolds' 1987 boids model: Separation (steer away from nearby boids), Alignment (match average velocity of neighbors), Cohesion (steer toward center of mass of neighbors). Three simple rules produce complex emergent flocking behavior.

questions.question: What makes A* faster than Dijkstra's algorithm?
options: A heuristic function guides search toward the goal
questions.correctAnswer: A heuristic function guides search toward the goal
questions.explanation: A* = Dijkstra + heuristic h(n). Dijkstra explores equally in all directions. A* uses h(n) (estimated distance to goal) to prioritize nodes closer to the goal. If h(n)=0, A* becomes Dijkstra. If h(n) is perfect, A* goes directly to the goal.

questions.question: What does PGA (Projective Geometric Algebra) unify?
options: Points, lines, planes, rotations, translations, and reflections in a single algebra
questions.correctAnswer: Points, lines, planes, rotations, translations, and reflections in a single algebra
questions.explanation: PGA (Plane-based GA) represents Euclidean geometry elegantly: points, lines, and planes are all elements of the same algebra. Rotations AND translations are both handled by rotors (the same sandwich product). No separate matrix for translation — translation is a rotor too.

questions.question: What is a rotor in geometric algebra?
options: The GA equivalent of a quaternion — represents rotation via double-sided product
A type of multivector for scaling
questions.correctAnswer: The GA equivalent of a quaternion — represents rotation via double-sided product
questions.explanation: A rotor R in GA rotates a vector v via the sandwich product: R v R~ (R with its reverse). This is identical to quaternion rotation but generalizes to any dimension. Rotors are elements of the even subalgebra.

questions.question: What is dead reckoning in networking?
options: Predicting a ship's position using celestial navigation
Extrapolating an object's position from its last known state (position + velocity)
Counting dead players in a multiplayer game
questions.correctAnswer: Extrapolating an object's position from its last known state (position + velocity)
questions.explanation: Dead reckoning: when no update arrives for a remote object, the client extrapolates: position += velocity * dt. When an update arrives, the client corrects to the authoritative position (with interpolation to smooth the correction). Used in every networked game.

questions.question: How does sound intensity change with distance?
options: Inverse square law — double distance, quarter intensity
Not at all — sound doesn't attenuate
questions.correctAnswer: Inverse square law — double distance, quarter intensity
questions.explanation: Sound follows the inverse square law: intensity ∝ 1/r². Double the distance = quarter the energy. In games, we often clamp this to a max distance for performance and use attenuation curves for artistic control.

questions.question: What is the computational complexity of FFT vs naive DFT?
options: DFT: O(n²), FFT: O(n log n)
DFT: O(n log n), FFT: O(n²)
questions.correctAnswer: DFT: O(n²), FFT: O(n log n)
questions.explanation: Naive DFT requires O(n²) operations — multiplying every input sample by every frequency. Cooley-Tukey FFT recursively splits into even/odd halves, achieving O(n log n). For n=1024: DFT = 1,048,576 ops, FFT = 10,240 ops — 100× faster.

questions.question: Why use Fisher-Yates instead of sort() with a random comparator for shuffling?
options: sort() with random comparator doesn't produce uniform permutations and violates strict weak ordering
questions.correctAnswer: sort() with random comparator doesn't produce uniform permutations and violates strict weak ordering
questions.explanation: sort() requires deterministic, transitive comparison. A random comparator violates both — causing undefined behavior and biased shuffles (some permutations more likely than others). Fisher-Yates is O(n) and produces every permutation with equal probability.

questions.question: What is the key property of a Poisson disc distribution?
options: No two points are closer than a minimum distance
questions.correctAnswer: No two points are closer than a minimum distance
questions.explanation: Poisson disc sampling ensures minimum distance between any two points, creating a natural-looking distribution without clumping. Used for: tree placement, foliage scattering, SSR ray origins. Also called blue noise sampling.

questions.question: What is octave noise (fractal noise)?
options: Summing multiple layers of noise at different frequencies and amplitudes
questions.correctAnswer: Summing multiple layers of noise at different frequencies and amplitudes
questions.explanation: Fractal/octave noise: sum multiple layers of noise, each with doubled frequency and halved amplitude (persistence). Layer 1: coarse terrain shape. Layer 2: medium hills. Layer 3: fine detail. The sum creates natural-looking landscapes.

questions.question: What does CCD stand for in inverse kinematics?
questions.correctAnswer: Cyclic Coordinate Descent
questions.explanation: CCD (Cyclic Coordinate Descent) is an IK algorithm: iteratively rotate each joint from the end effector backward to minimize distance to the target. Simple, robust, works for any number of joints. FABRIK is the other common IK algorithm.

questions.question: What is the lerp delta-time trap?
options: Using lerp makes objects move too fast
Using lerp with a fixed ratio each frame makes movement frame-rate dependent
questions.correctAnswer: Using lerp with a fixed ratio each frame makes movement frame-rate dependent
questions.explanation: lerp(a, b, 0.1) each frame: at 60fps, the object moves 0.1 of the remaining distance 60 times/sec. At 30fps, only 30 times/sec. Different frame rates = different speeds. Fix: use exponential decay based on dt: lerp(a, b, 1 - exp(-speed * dt)).

questions.question: What is the 'candy-wrapper' effect in skeletal animation?
options: Linear blend skinning causing volume collapse at joints
questions.correctAnswer: Linear blend skinning causing volume collapse at joints
questions.explanation: LBS (Linear Blend Skinning) averages bone transforms weighted by vertex weights. When two bones rotate in opposite directions (e.g., elbow bend), the weighted average collapses — like twisting a candy wrapper. Dual quaternion skinning fixes this by using quaternion blending instead of matrix averaging.

questions.question: How many possible configurations does the Marching Cubes lookup table have?
questions.explanation: Marching Cubes classifies each of the 8 corners of a voxel as inside or outside (2^8 = 256 cases). The lookup table maps each case to a set of triangles. Vertex positions are interpolated along edges where the density field crosses zero.

questions.question: What does a signed distance field (SDF) return for a point inside a surface?
questions.correctAnswer: A negative value
questions.explanation: SDF: f(p) = signed distance to nearest surface. Inside surface → negative. On surface → zero. Outside → positive. The gradient of the SDF gives the surface normal. Raymarching: step forward by f(p) — this is the MAXIMUM safe distance you can advance without missing a surface.

questions.question: Why do rendering calculations happen in linear color space?
options: Because lighting is physically linear — gamma-corrected values produce incorrect results
Linear space is faster to compute
questions.correctAnswer: Because lighting is physically linear — gamma-corrected values produce incorrect results
questions.explanation: Lighting is additive: two photons = twice the brightness. Gamma encoding (sRGB ~2.2) is a nonlinear curve for display. Doing lighting in sRGB space produces incorrect results. Textures must be linearized (sRGB→linear), shaded, then re-encoded (linear→sRGB).

questions.question: What is an octree?
options: A spatial data structure that recursively subdivides 3D space into 8 children
questions.correctAnswer: A spatial data structure that recursively subdivides 3D space into 8 children
questions.explanation: An octree subdivides 3D space: each node has 8 child octants (one per corner of the cube). Used for: sparse voxel storage, spatial queries, level-of-detail rendering. Empty/solid regions aren't subdivided, saving ~90% memory vs dense grids.

questions.question: What is an FInterpCurve?
options: A keyframe-based curve that interpolates between points
questions.correctAnswer: A keyframe-based curve that interpolates between points
questions.explanation: FInterpCurve<T> stores keyframes (FInterpCurvePoint<T>) with: InVal (time), OutVal (value), ArriveTangent, LeaveTangent, InterpMode. Eval(time) finds bracketing keyframes and interpolates. Supports linear, cubic, and constant interpolation modes.

questions.question: What UE primitive does FBox represent?
questions.correctAnswer: An Axis-Aligned Bounding Box (AABB)
questions.explanation: FBox is an AABB defined by Min and Max points. UE uses FBox for: broad-phase collision detection, spatial hashing, culling. FBox::Intersect(FBox) checks if two boxes overlap.

questions.question: What does FMath::Lerp do?
options: Clamps a value between min and max
Linear interpolation between A and B by alpha
questions.correctAnswer: Linear interpolation between A and B by alpha
questions.explanation: FMath::Lerp(A, B, Alpha) returns A + (B-A)*Alpha. At Alpha=0 → A, Alpha=1 → B. FMath::LerpStable handles edge cases where Alpha is outside [0,1] and ensures consistent behavior.

questions.question: What does FTransform store?
questions.correctAnswer: Position, rotation (FQuat), and scale (FVector)
questions.explanation: FTransform stores: Translation (FVector), Rotation (FQuat), Scale3D (FVector). This is 3+4+3 = 10 floats (40 bytes). FTransform is preferred over FMatrix for scene objects because quaternions avoid gimbal lock and are smaller.

note: FVector, FRotator, FQuat — The Core Types

questions.question: What is FVector in UE5?
questions.correctAnswer: A double-precision 3D vector (UE::Math::TVector<double>)
questions.explanation: UE5 uses double-precision vectors by default (FVector = UE::Math::TVector<double>). This was changed from UE4's float-based FVector to support large world coordinates (LWC).

questions.question: What is CMake?
options: A build system generator — it generates native build files (Makefiles, VS solutions) from a platform-independent script
questions.correctAnswer: A build system generator — it generates native build files (Makefiles, VS solutions) from a platform-independent script
questions.explanation: Note: CMake is a meta-build system. It reads CMakeLists.txt and generates platform-specific build files.

questions.question: What is stack unwinding?
options: When an exception is thrown, destructors of all local objects are called in reverse order as the stack unwinds to find a catch handler
questions.correctAnswer: When an exception is thrown, destructors of all local objects are called in reverse order as the stack unwinds to find a catch handler
questions.explanation: Note: stack unwinding is the process of destroying local objects as the runtime searches up the call stack for a matching catch block.

questions.question: What is the primary advantage of C++20 modules over #include?
options: Dramatically faster compilation — modules are pre-compiled, not text-pasted
questions.correctAnswer: Dramatically faster compilation — modules are pre-compiled, not text-pasted
questions.explanation: Note: modules avoid the repeated text parsing of headers. 'import std;' replaces including the entire standard library.

questions.question: What are the four stages of C++ compilation in order?
questions.correctAnswer: Preprocessor, Compiler, Assembler, Linker
questions.explanation: Note shows pipeline: .cpp → preprocessor (.i) → compiler (.s) → assembler (.o) → linker (.exe).

questions.question: Why does UE disable C++ exceptions?
options: Performance predictability, code size, and console platform constraints
questions.correctAnswer: Performance predictability, code size, and console platform constraints
questions.explanation: Note: UE replaces exceptions with check()/ensure()/verify() macros that are more predictable with smaller codegen impact.

questions.question: What does deducing this (C++23) eliminate?
options: The CRTP pattern — no more static_cast<Derived*>(this)
questions.correctAnswer: The CRTP pattern — no more static_cast<Derived*>(this)
questions.explanation: Note: 'Deducing this kills the CRTP pattern. void f(this Self&& self) deduces the derived type automatically.'

questions.question: What is the key advantage of ranges over traditional iterator-pair algorithms?
options: Composable pipelines with lazy evaluation — filter, transform, take chain without intermediate allocations
questions.correctAnswer: Composable pipelines with lazy evaluation — filter, transform, take chain without intermediate allocations
questions.explanation: Note: 'Ranges compose with | (pipe). filter | transform | take — zero intermediate allocations, lazy evaluation.'

questions.question: What are the three keywords that identify C++20 coroutines?
questions.correctAnswer: co_await, co_yield, co_return
questions.explanation: Note discusses co_await (suspend and resume), co_yield (produce a value and suspend), and co_return (complete the coroutine).

questions.question: What is the key difference between std::variant and a C union?
options: variant has a discriminator tracking which alternative is active — type-safe; C unions don't track active member
questions.correctAnswer: variant has a discriminator tracking which alternative is active — type-safe; C unions don't track active member
questions.explanation: Note: 'variant adds a discriminator tracking the active alternative. A C union doesn't track which member is active.'

questions.question: Does std::optional allocate on the heap?
options: No — stores the value inline, sizeof approximately sizeof(T) + alignment padding
questions.correctAnswer: No — stores the value inline, sizeof approximately sizeof(T) + alignment padding
questions.explanation: Note: 'optional is NOT a pointer. No heap allocation. Stores the value inline in its own footprint.'

questions.question: Why does marking a move constructor noexcept improve std::vector performance?
options: vector uses move_if_noexcept: noexcept move = pointer swap; non-noexcept = forced copy on reallocation for exception safety
questions.correctAnswer: vector uses move_if_noexcept: noexcept move = pointer swap; non-noexcept = forced copy on reallocation for exception safety
questions.explanation: Note: 'vector checks noexcept. Yes → move (pointer swap, ~3 cycles). No → copy (deep copy, thousands of cycles).'

questions.question: What is the key difference between const and constexpr?
options: const is a runtime promise (won't modify); constexpr guarantees compile-time evaluation
questions.correctAnswer: const is a runtime promise (won't modify); constexpr guarantees compile-time evaluation
questions.explanation: Note: 'const promises I won't write to this. constexpr guarantees this value is known at compile time.'

questions.question: What does consteval (C++20) enforce?
options: MUST be evaluated at compile time — error if called with runtime arguments
questions.correctAnswer: MUST be evaluated at compile time — error if called with runtime arguments
questions.explanation: Note: 'consteval creates an immediate function — every call MUST produce a compile-time constant.'

questions.question: What does std::move actually do?
options: Casts its argument to an rvalue reference, enabling move semantics
questions.correctAnswer: Casts its argument to an rvalue reference, enabling move semantics
questions.explanation: Note: 'std::move is a cast. It doesn't move anything — it casts to T&&, enabling the move constructor to be selected.'

questions.question: What is the difference between std::move and std::forward?
options: move unconditionally casts to rvalue; forward conditionally casts based on template argument deduction
move is for unique_ptr; forward is for shared_ptr
questions.correctAnswer: move unconditionally casts to rvalue; forward conditionally casts based on template argument deduction
questions.explanation: Note: move always casts to rvalue reference. forward preserves the value category (lvalue stays lvalue).

questions.question: When are templates instantiated?
options: At compile time, at the point of use — the compiler generates a separate copy for each type
questions.correctAnswer: At compile time, at the point of use — the compiler generates a separate copy for each type
questions.explanation: Note: 'Templates are instantiated at the point of use. The compiler needs the full definition visible.'

questions.question: What problem do C++20 concepts solve?
options: Incomprehensible template error messages — concepts produce clean errors at the call site
questions.correctAnswer: Incomprehensible template error messages — concepts produce clean errors at the call site
questions.explanation: Note: 'The biggest problem with templates has always been error messages. Pass the wrong type and get 300 lines of trace. Concepts fix this.'

questions.question: Which operators MUST be member functions (not non-member)?
questions.explanation: Note: 'The rule: =, [], (), -> MUST be members. Others typically non-member for symmetry.'

questions.question: What does the spaceship operator <=> (C++20) generate?
questions.correctAnswer: All six comparison operators: <, <=, >, >=, ==, !=
questions.explanation: Note: 'One operator gives you ALL comparisons automatically. Generates ==, !=, <, <=, >, >= from comparing members.'

questions.question: Which cast performs a safe, runtime-checked downcast?
questions.correctAnswer: dynamic_cast
questions.explanation: Note: 'dynamic_cast checks at runtime whether the cast is valid by walking the class hierarchy. Returns nullptr (pointer) or throws (reference) on failure.'

questions.question: What does static_cast do for downcasts?
options: No runtime check — compiles silently, undefined behavior if wrong type
questions.correctAnswer: No runtime check — compiles silently, undefined behavior if wrong type
questions.explanation: Note warns: 'static_cast downcasts are dangerous. No runtime check — if base_ptr is actually Base, not Derived, undefined behavior.'

questions.question: What does the capture list [] in a lambda control?
options: Which external variables the lambda can access and how (by value or reference)
questions.correctAnswer: Which external variables the lambda can access and how (by value or reference)
questions.explanation: Note: 'The capture list controls which variables from the enclosing scope the lambda can access.' [=] copies, [&] references.

questions.question: What is the danger of [&] capture in asynchronous lambdas?
options: Captured references may dangle if the lambda outlives the referenced variables
questions.correctAnswer: Captured references may dangle if the lambda outlives the referenced variables
questions.explanation: Note warns: 'Capturing by Reference Is Dangerous. If the referenced variable is destroyed before the lambda runs, you have a dangling reference.'

questions.question: What does the virtual keyword enable?
options: Runtime dispatch — the function called depends on the actual object type, not the pointer type
questions.correctAnswer: Runtime dispatch — the function called depends on the actual object type, not the pointer type
questions.explanation: Note: 'virtual tells the compiler: Don't decide which function to call based on the pointer type. Look at the actual object at runtime.'

questions.question: What does override do and why should you always use it?
options: Compiler verifies the function actually overrides a base class virtual function — catches typos and signature mismatches
It prevents the function from being called
questions.correctAnswer: Compiler verifies the function actually overrides a base class virtual function — catches typos and signature mismatches
questions.explanation: Note: 'Always use override. It catches typos, wrong parameter types, wrong const qualifiers, and missing virtual in base.'

questions.question: Why must a base class with virtual functions have a virtual destructor?
options: So delete through a base pointer calls the correct derived destructor chain
questions.correctAnswer: So delete through a base pointer calls the correct derived destructor chain
questions.explanation: Note: 'If a class has ANY virtual function, make its destructor virtual. Without it, delete through base pointer only calls base destructor — leak.'

questions.question: What is the key difference between std::vector<int> and std::pmr::vector<int>?
options: pmr::vector uses a runtime polymorphic allocator — same type regardless of where memory comes from; regular vector's allocator is a template parameter
questions.correctAnswer: pmr::vector uses a runtime polymorphic allocator — same type regardless of where memory comes from; regular vector's allocator is a template parameter
questions.explanation: Note: pmr decouples the allocator from the type. All pmr::vector<int> instances share the same type.

questions.question: What is a monotonic_buffer_resource?
options: An arena allocator — pre-allocated buffer, bump-pointer allocation, freed all at once on destruction
questions.correctAnswer: An arena allocator — pre-allocated buffer, bump-pointer allocation, freed all at once on destruction
questions.explanation: monotonic_buffer_resource is a bump allocator. Individual deallocations are ignored; the entire buffer is reclaimed when the resource is destroyed.

questions.question: How does std::deque achieve O(1) push_front and push_back?
options: Array of pointers to fixed-size blocks (chunked array)
questions.correctAnswer: Array of pointers to fixed-size blocks (chunked array)
questions.explanation: deque uses a chunked array: a map of pointers to fixed-size blocks. Adding at either end allocates a new block as needed.

questions.question: Which container adaptor provides FIFO (first-in-first-out) behavior?
questions.correctAnswer: std::queue
questions.explanation: std::queue adapts a container (default deque) to provide FIFO: push at back, pop from front.

questions.question: What does std::filesystem::path::stem() return for 'archive.tar.gz'?
questions.correctAnswer: archive.tar
questions.explanation: stem() removes only the last extension. 'archive.tar.gz' → stem = 'archive.tar', extension = '.gz'.

questions.question: What does fs::create_directories() do?
questions.correctAnswer: Creates all intermediate directories (like mkdir -p)
questions.explanation: create_directories creates the entire path, including any missing parent directories.

questions.question: What is std::span?
options: A non-owning view over contiguous data — pointer + size, zero allocation
questions.correctAnswer: A non-owning view over contiguous data — pointer + size, zero allocation
questions.explanation: Note: 'span is a non-owning view over a contiguous sequence. It stores exactly a pointer and a size. No allocation.'

questions.question: What is the difference between std::span<T> and std::span<T, N>?
options: span<T> has dynamic extent (size stored at runtime, 16 bytes); span<T,N> has static extent (size in type, 8 bytes)
questions.correctAnswer: span<T> has dynamic extent (size stored at runtime, 16 bytes); span<T,N> has static extent (size in type, 8 bytes)
questions.explanation: Static-extent spans store only the pointer — the size is a compile-time constant, saving 8 bytes.

questions.question: What are the five iterator categories in C++?
questions.correctAnswer: Input, Output, Forward, Bidirectional, Random Access
acceptableAnswers: input output forward bidirectional random access
questions.explanation: Note lists five iterator categories: input (read-once), output (write-once), forward (multi-pass), bidirectional (--), random access ([] and +n).

questions.question: What does std::sort require from its iterators?
questions.correctAnswer: Random access iterators
questions.explanation: std::sort requires random access iterators because it needs O(1) element access for partitioning.

questions.question: What data structure does std::map use internally?
questions.correctAnswer: Red-black tree (self-balancing binary search tree)
questions.explanation: std::map is ordered and uses a balanced BST. O(log n) for all operations.

questions.question: What data structure does std::unordered_map use?
questions.correctAnswer: Hash table with chaining
questions.explanation: std::unordered_map uses hashing. O(1) average, O(n) worst case for lookups.

questions.question: What three pointers does std::vector store internally?
questions.correctAnswer: begin, end (size), end of storage (capacity)
questions.explanation: Note: vector stores begin pointer, end pointer (one past last element), and end-of-capacity pointer. sizeof(vector) = 24 bytes on 64-bit.

questions.question: What is the growth factor used by most std::vector implementations?
questions.correctAnswer: 1.5x (MSVC) or 2x (GCC/Clang)
questions.explanation: Note: GCC/libc++ use 2x; MSVC uses 1.5x. Must be exponential for amortized O(1) push_back.

questions.question: What is the key advantage of std::array over a C-style array?
options: Same memory layout but adds .size(), .at() bounds checking, and never decays to pointer
Automatic bounds checking on every operator[]
questions.correctAnswer: Same memory layout but adds .size(), .at() bounds checking, and never decays to pointer
questions.explanation: std::array is zero-overhead: same contiguous memory as C array, but with container interface.

questions.question: What happens to a C array's size information when passed to a function?
options: It decays to a pointer — sizeof becomes pointer size (8 bytes), array size is lost
It's passed as a hidden second parameter
questions.correctAnswer: It decays to a pointer — sizeof becomes pointer size (8 bytes), array size is lost
questions.explanation: C arrays decay to pointers. sizeof(arr) in a function returns 8 (pointer size), not the array size.

questions.question: What is the key safety advantage of std::format over printf?
options: Format string type mismatches are caught at compile time, not runtime
questions.correctAnswer: Format string type mismatches are caught at compile time, not runtime
questions.explanation: Note: 'Errors in your format string are caught at compile time — not at 3 AM when a player triggers a rare dialogue path.'

questions.question: How do you make a custom type work with std::format?
options: Specialize std::formatter<T> with constexpr parse() and format() methods
questions.correctAnswer: Specialize std::formatter<T> with constexpr parse() and format() methods
questions.explanation: Note: 'Custom types integrate via template specialization of std::formatter<T> with a constexpr parse() and a format() method.'

questions.question: What does RAII stand for?
questions.correctAnswer: Resource Acquisition Is Initialization
questions.explanation: Note: 'RAII means tie the lifetime of a resource to the lifetime of an object. Constructor acquires, destructor releases.'

questions.question: What are the five special member functions in the Rule of Five?
questions.correctAnswer: Destructor, copy constructor, copy assignment, move constructor, move assignment
acceptableAnswers: destructor copy constructor copy assignment move constructor move assignment
~T(), T(const T&), T& operator=(const T&), T(T&&), T& operator=(T&&)
questions.explanation: Rule of Five: if you define any of the five, you should explicitly define all of them for consistent resource management.

questions.question: Why does the Rule of Five exist?
options: If you need a custom destructor (resource ownership), compiler-generated copy/move might do shallow copies of your resource
questions.correctAnswer: If you need a custom destructor (resource ownership), compiler-generated copy/move might do shallow copies of your resource
questions.explanation: A custom destructor signals resource ownership. Default copy/move would copy the pointer, not the resource — double-free or leak.

questions.question: What is the Rule of Zero?
options: Don't declare any special members — use RAII types (unique_ptr, vector, string) as members so compiler-generated ones are correct
questions.correctAnswer: Don't declare any special members — use RAII types (unique_ptr, vector, string) as members so compiler-generated ones are correct
questions.explanation: Rule of Zero: if all members are RAII types, the compiler-generated destructor/copy/move are already correct.

questions.question: In what three situations are destructors guaranteed to run in C++?
questions.correctAnswer: Normal scope exit, exception unwinding, and early return/break/continue
questions.explanation: Note lists three guarantees: normal scope exit, exception unwinding (stack unwinding), and any control flow leaving scope.

questions.question: Why don't raw pointers auto-clean heap memory when they go out of scope?
options: The pointer variable (8 bytes holding the address) is destroyed, but the heap object it pointed to is NOT
questions.correctAnswer: The pointer variable (8 bytes holding the address) is destroyed, but the heap object it pointed to is NOT
questions.explanation: Note: 'When a pointer goes out of scope, the pointer VARIABLE is destroyed. But the memory it points to on the heap is NOT.'

questions.question: What RAII principle do smart pointers implement?
options: Tie resource lifetime to object lifetime — destructor automatically frees the managed object
questions.correctAnswer: Tie resource lifetime to object lifetime — destructor automatically frees the managed object
questions.explanation: Smart pointer destructor automatically deletes the managed object when the smart pointer itself goes out of scope.

questions.question: What is unique about std::unique_ptr ownership?
options: Only one unique_ptr can own the object at a time — can't be copied, only moved
It can only point to stack objects
questions.correctAnswer: Only one unique_ptr can own the object at a time — can't be copied, only moved
questions.explanation: unique_ptr enforces exclusive ownership. Copy is deleted; only move is allowed.

questions.question: When should you use std::shared_ptr over std::unique_ptr?
options: When multiple owners need to share the same object, and the last one to die cleans it up
questions.correctAnswer: When multiple owners need to share the same object, and the last one to die cleans it up
questions.explanation: shared_ptr uses reference counting. Object deleted when the last shared_ptr is destroyed.

questions.question: What is std::weak_ptr used for?
options: Breaking reference cycles in shared_ptr graphs — it doesn't affect the reference count
questions.correctAnswer: Breaking reference cycles in shared_ptr graphs — it doesn't affect the reference count
questions.explanation: weak_ptr observes a shared_ptr's object without owning it. Essential for breaking circular references.

questions.question: Why is sizeof(struct{char a; int b; char c;}) typically 12, not 6?
options: Padding — CPU requires aligned access; compiler inserts invisible bytes
questions.correctAnswer: Padding — CPU requires aligned access; compiler inserts invisible bytes
questions.explanation: Note: 'Six bytes of data, twelve bytes of object. Padding. A 4-byte int must live at an address divisible by 4.'

questions.question: What hardware constraint drives alignment requirements?
options: CPUs read memory in aligned word chunks — misaligned data crossing a word boundary requires two reads
questions.correctAnswer: CPUs read memory in aligned word chunks — misaligned data crossing a word boundary requires two reads
questions.explanation: Note: CPUs don't read byte-by-byte. A misaligned int crossing a word boundary needs two memory accesses.

questions.question: How can you reduce padding in a struct?
options: Order members from largest to smallest alignment
questions.correctAnswer: Order members from largest to smallest alignment
questions.explanation: Placing larger-alignment members first reduces padding gaps between members.

questions.question: What is the alignment of a double on x86-64?
questions.correctAnswer: 8 bytes
questions.explanation: Note: 'A type of size N must live at an address divisible by N (for fundamental types up to pointer size).' double is 8 bytes.

questions.question: What does AoS vs SoA stand for and which is faster for SIMD?
options: Allocation of Structures vs Structures of Allocation
Array of Structures vs Structure of Arrays — SoA is often faster for SIMD
questions.correctAnswer: Array of Structures vs Structure of Arrays — SoA is often faster for SIMD
questions.explanation: Structure of Arrays keeps same-type fields contiguous for better cache and SIMD utilization.

questions.question: What is the fundamental performance difference between stack and heap allocation?
options: Stack: one CPU instruction (move stack pointer); heap: searching for free space, ~50-200+ cycles
Stack is slower due to LIFO
questions.correctAnswer: Stack: one CPU instruction (move stack pointer); heap: searching for free space, ~50-200+ cycles
questions.explanation: Note states stack allocation is automatic, one CPU instruction. Heap involves searching for free space.

questions.question: What determines the lifetime of a stack variable?
options: Tied to function scope — destroyed when the function returns
questions.correctAnswer: Tied to function scope — destroyed when the function returns
questions.explanation: Stack lifetime is automatic — popped when function returns. Heap: manual via delete.

questions.question: What is the typical maximum stack size?
questions.correctAnswer: 1-8 MB (platform-dependent)
questions.explanation: Stack ~1-8 MB. Heap: gigabytes. Large data must go on the heap.

questions.question: What register tracks the top of the stack on x86-64?
questions.correctAnswer: RSP
questions.explanation: Note: 'The CPU tracks the top with a register called the stack pointer (RSP on x86-64).'

questions.question: Why is the stack cache-friendly?
options: It's LIFO — the top is always in L1/L2 cache, recently used memory is hot
questions.correctAnswer: It's LIFO — the top is always in L1/L2 cache, recently used memory is hot
questions.explanation: The stack's LIFO nature means the most recently pushed data sits at the top, which stays cache-hot.

note: Applied Examples - Raw C++ vs Unreal Engine

questions.question: In UE, what container should you use when you need fast iteration AND frequent insertions/removals?
options: TArray for iteration, TMap for lookups; profile before choosing anything else
questions.correctAnswer: TArray for iteration, TMap for lookups; profile before choosing anything else
questions.explanation: TArray (contiguous) is cache-optimal for iteration. TMap (hash table) is O(1) for lookups. TLinkedList is almost never the right choice — pointer chasing destroys cache locality. Profile before deviating from TArray/TMap.

questions.question: Why is std::vector::push_back O(1) amortized despite occasional reallocation?
options: The geometric growth (2× expansion) means the cost of N pushes is O(N), so average is O(1)
questions.correctAnswer: The geometric growth (2× expansion) means the cost of N pushes is O(N), so average is O(1)
questions.explanation: Amortized analysis: total cost of N pushes = N + N/2 + N/4 + ... ≈ 2N. Average = 2N/N = O(1). The geometric growth factor (×2) is critical — a fixed growth (+K) would give O(n) amortized push_back.

questions.question: What is the Big O of binary search?
questions.correctAnswer: O(log n)
questions.explanation: Binary search halves the search space each step: O(log₂ n). For n=1,000,000, binary search takes ~20 steps. Linear search takes up to 1,000,000. This is why sorted data structures enable dramatically faster lookups.

questions.question: What is the GAS (Gameplay Ability System) prediction model?
options: Client predicts ability activation, server corrects if prediction was wrong
No prediction — everything runs on server
questions.correctAnswer: Client predicts ability activation, server corrects if prediction was wrong
questions.explanation: GAS uses client-side prediction: abilities execute locally first (immediate feedback), then the server validates and corrects if needed. Similar to movement prediction in the CharacterMovementComponent. Prediction keys track which activations the server has confirmed.

questions.question: How does the spatial grid filter in Iris achieve O(1) per-pair filtering?
options: By checking distance for each pair
By precomputing object cell indices and connection visible cell sets — filter check is cell membership test
questions.correctAnswer: By precomputing object cell indices and connection visible cell sets — filter check is cell membership test
questions.explanation: Grid filter: world divided into cells (~50-100m). Objects register their cell. Connections have a precomputed visible cell set. Filter check: is object's cell in connection's visible set? O(1) set lookup instead of O(n) distance checks.

questions.question: What is Iris in UE5?
options: UE5's new replication system replacing the legacy actor channel model
questions.correctAnswer: UE5's new replication system replacing the legacy actor channel model
questions.explanation: Iris is UE5's next-generation replication system. It replaces the per-actor channel model with a data-driven architecture: dirty properties flow through filtering → prioritization → serialization pipelines. Designed for large-scale games (100+ players, 1000+ replicated objects).

questions.question: What performance problem does PushModel solve?
options: Traditional replication compares all properties every frame — PushModel only replicates explicitly marked dirty properties
questions.correctAnswer: Traditional replication compares all properties every frame — PushModel only replicates explicitly marked dirty properties
questions.explanation: PushModel: properties are only replicated when explicitly marked dirty via MARK_PROPERTY_DIRTY_FROM_NAME(). This eliminates per-frame property comparison for properties that rarely change. Up to 60% CPU savings on the server replication path.

questions.question: What does RepNotify do?
options: Calls a callback function when a replicated property's value arrives
Notifies the server of a property change
questions.correctAnswer: Calls a callback function when a replicated property's value arrives
questions.explanation: RepNotify: when a UPROPERTY(ReplicatedUsing=OnRep_Health) changes on the server, the new value replicates to clients, and OnRep_Health() is called automatically. Use to: play effects, update UI, trigger animations on value changes.

questions.question: Which RPC type is called on the server and executed on ALL clients?
questions.correctAnswer: NetMulticast RPC
questions.explanation: NetMulticast: called on server, executed on server AND all connected clients. Server: called from client, executed only on server. Client: called from server, executed only on the owning client.

questions.question: What are the three authority roles in UE networking?
questions.correctAnswer: Authority (server), Autonomous Proxy (owning client), Simulated Proxy (other clients)
questions.explanation: Authority: runs on server, has full control. Autonomous Proxy: on the owning client's machine, can send RPCs and inputs. Simulated Proxy: on other clients, receives replicated state only. Each role determines what can execute on that instance.

questions.question: What does ExactCast<T> do?
options: Only succeeds if the object's class is EXACTLY T — no derived classes
Casts to the exact pointer size
questions.correctAnswer: Only succeeds if the object's class is EXACTLY T — no derived classes
